accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
• Classify dizziness phenotype: vertigo, presyncope/light-headedness, or disequilibrium.
  • Define onset, duration, triggers and recurrence pattern.
  • Perform focused neurological and gait examination for central signs.
  • Assess hearing loss, tinnitus and aural fullness.
  • Review medicines and check postural blood pressure where appropriate.
  • Perform Hallpike test when indicated and trained.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
• Treat confirmed posterior-canal BPPV with canalith repositioning (e.g. Epley).
  • Use vestibular suppressants short term only in severe acute symptoms.
  • Provide falls and activity safety advice and encourage graded mobilisation.
  • Arrange timely reassessment if symptoms do not improve or diagnosis is uncertain.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Hospital admission (same day)

  • Acute dizziness/vertigo with focal neurological deficit.
  • Acute vestibular syndrome with concern for central cause.
  • Severe new gait ataxia, reduced consciousness, persistent vomiting/dehydration, or severe new headache.

Urgent suspicion of cancer (USOC)

  • No routine USOC trigger in isolated dizziness; use cancer pathway if separate red-flag features are present.

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • High-risk atypical presentations requiring rapid specialist assessment where central pathology remains possible.

Routine (outpatient)

  • BPPV persisting despite appropriate repositioning manoeuvres.
  • Persistent dizziness/vertigo beyond around 6 weeks despite first-line care.
  • Recurrent vestibular migraine or Menière-type episodes not controlled in primary care.

Include in referral

  • Symptom profile, trigger pattern, and functional impact.
  • Neurological/otological findings, Hallpike result, and treatment already given.
  • Medication review and orthostatic BP findings.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html: • Do not routinely refer low-risk peripheral dizziness/vertigo that is improving with primary-care treatment and has no neurological red flags.

accordion_sections.heading: References
accordion_sections.html:
1. NICE NG127 dizziness/vertigo-related thresholds.
  2. Right Decisions and territorial board dizziness pathways (NHS Borders, NHS Lothian, NHS GGC).
  3. NHS inform dizziness/vertigo information.
